title: user message
summary: **Constraints:** - Use only one sub-structure. **Goal:** - Build an extremely complex and visually ornate bridge using only one sub-structure. - The design should showcase as many block types as possible to maximize art…
body:
**Constraints:**
- Use only one sub-structure.

**Goal:**
- Build an extremely complex and visually ornate bridge using only one sub-structure.
- The design should showcase as many block types as possible to maximize artistic expression and structural complexity.
- The bridge must be capable of spanning a 10-unit gap between two flat terrains.
- The bridge deck must be at least 5 units above the bottom of the gap.

**Evaluation Protocol:**
- The terrains are positioned to form a 10-unit-wide gap along the north–south axis.
- The bridge will be initially placed above the gap so it can gently fall into position.
- There will be no fixed connection between the bridge and the terrain.
- The bridge must successfully span the full 10-unit gap using only one sub-structure.
- The bridge deck must remain at a height of at least 5 units above the bottom of the gap.
- The structure will be evaluated not only for successful spanning, but also for its visual richness, use of diverse block types, and overall architectural complexity.
- Designs that fail to span the gap, collapse immediately, or do not satisfy the deck-height requirement will receive a score of 0.

**Scoring Metrics:**
- *Structural Validity:* Whether the bridge successfully spans the 10-unit gap with a deck height of at least 5 units.
- *Aesthetic Complexity:* Degree of visual intricacy, ornamentation, and architectural sophistication.
- *Block Variety:* Number and diversity of block types used in the design (more is better).
- *Cost:* Number of blocks used to build the bridge.

title: planner message
summary: <building_plan> <overall_structure> <description> A single-piece, self-supporting, through-truss ornamental bridge that spans north–south. The main deck is a rigid 3-wide by 13-long lattice of Small Wooden Blocks (with…
body:
<building_plan>
  <overall_structure>
    <description>
      A single-piece, self-supporting, through-truss ornamental bridge that spans north–south. The main deck is a rigid 3-wide by 13-long lattice of Small Wooden Blocks (with the single default stone base block embedded at midspan), ensuring a clear span of 10 units with comfortable overhang at each end. Along both east and west edges, two-tier parapet posts rise 2 units above the deck, carrying a visually rich arrangement of alternating Water Cannons (as sculptural “ribs” tilted inward) and Torch lanterns (oriented outward, safely clear of flammables). Below the deck, Powered Wheels are mounted on alternating bays as large medallions, creating depth and visual rhythm without interfering with the walking surface. 
      
      Aesthetic complexity is enhanced with:
      - Alternating Water Cannon “arch ribs” and Torch lanterns along both parapets.
      - Powered Wheel medallions suspended just below the deck edges on alternating bays.
      - Longitudinal and diagonal Brace patterns forming through-truss lattices along both sides and cross-bracing across the width at select bays.
      - A small, central pylon rising from the center of the deck with a short cross-beam, from which Winches create decorative, taut “suspenders” to various deck points (and/or side posts) to suggest a catenary motif.
      
      The whole bridge is built as a single sub-structure and will be placed above the north–south gap to settle gently onto both sides without any fixed terrain connection.
    </description>
    <functionality>
      - Structural validity: The deck’s overall length is 13 units, exceeding the 10-unit gap. This creates ≥1.5-unit overhang on each side for robust bearing on the two opposing terrain edges when the bridge is lowered into place. A 3-block width (east–west) improves torsional stability.
      - Deck height: The deck is the top surface of the 3×13 wooden grid. With two-tier parapets and rigid block attachments, the deck stays near terrain edge elevation; it will not sag into the gap. Because it rests on the edges and the gap’s bottom is below, the deck remains at least 5 units above the bottom (as typical terrain step-downs are larger than 5); the rigid, wide deck plus trussed sides and cross-bracing preserve deck elevation after settling.
      - Block variety and ornamentation: All available block types are employed as part of the decorative and structural language:
        - Small Wooden Blocks: primary deck, parapets, pylon, and anchor “cleats.”
        - Powered Wheels: ornamental rosettes, alternating below deck edges.
        - Water Cannons: sculptural arch ribs on parapets, angled inward; left OFF during evaluation.
        - Torches: outward-facing lanterns, carefully positioned so their 0.3-radius heating sphere is in free air and does not ignite wood.
        - Braces: longitudinal top-chord ties, side diagonals, and cross-bracing for visual lattice and added rigidity.
        - Winches: decorative suspenders from the central pylon to the deck and/or parapets, adding complexity and helping restrain internal motion.
      - Safety of flammables and actives:
        - Torches are oriented so their heating sphere does not intersect wood or Water Cannons (minimum center-to-wood distance ≥0.5 > 0.3). 
        - Water Cannons remain unfired (no recoil), so they serve as inert sculptures; they are spaced so no steam-heating risk occurs from torches.
    </functionality>
    <assembly>
      The single sub-structure is assembled in layered steps around the default 1×1×1 stone base block at the geometric center of the deck (y=0):
      1) Main deck, 3-wide by 13-long:
         - Place the default stone block at the center of the deck (x=0, y=0, z=0). 
         - Build the central longitudinal row (x=0) by attaching 6 Small Wooden Blocks successively to the north face and 6 to the south face of the central stone, yielding a 13-block-long center line along y ∈ {−6,…,0,…,+6}. 
         - For each of these 13 center-line positions, attach one Small Wooden Block to the east face (x=+1) and one to the west face (x=−1) of the center row block, creating a 3-wide deck grid. This produces a 3×13 deck (39 wooden blocks) with the stone keystone embedded at midspan.
         - End “feet”: Under each of the six deck blocks at the extreme ends (y=±6, for x=−1, 0, +1), attach one Small Wooden Block to the bottom face to create thicker end pads for better contact with terrain edges.
      2) Parapet posts (two tiers) along both edges:
         - On every east-edge deck block (x=+1, all y), attach one Small Wooden Block on its top face (z=+1), then attach a second on top of the first (z=+2). Repeat for every west-edge deck block (x=−1). This yields a continuous double-height post line on both sides (2 tiers × 13 positions × 2 sides = 52 Small Wooden Blocks).
      3) Decorative Powered Wheel “medallions” below the deck (alternate bays):
         - On the bottom face of east-edge deck blocks at odd y positions (y=±1, ±3, ±5), attach one Small Wooden Block as a medallion base (center at z=−1). On the outer face of each such base block (east face), attach a Powered Wheel so its disc plane is vertical (YZ plane) and its thickness extends outward (+x). Repeat symmetrically on the west edge (bottom face base block at odd y, attach wheel to the west face).
         - Spacing: with odd-integer y spacing (2 units apart), 2-unit center spacing prevents any adjacency overlap along y. The wheel disc (diameter 2) sits largely below the deck (top edge near deck bottom), clear of posts and walkway.
      4) Alternating Water Cannons and Torch lanterns on parapets:
         - On the top post of each bay, use the OUTER vertical face (east face on the east parapet, west face on the west parapet) for sculptural attachments. Alternate by bay:
           - Even y (… −6, −4, −2, 0, 2, 4, 6): On the east parapet top posts, attach a Water Cannon to the outer face, oriented inward and upward (tilted ≈30–45° toward the centerline), forming an “arch rib” look above the deck. On the west parapet top posts at even y, attach a Torch to the outer face, oriented upward and outward. 
           - Odd y (… −5, −3, −1, 1, 3, 5): Reverse the pattern—Water Cannons on the west parapet outer faces, Torches on the east parapet outer faces.
         - Torch safety: Each Torch’s flame center sits ~0.5 units outboard from the post face and 1 unit above the mount; its 0.3-unit heating radius stays in free air. Nearest wood is ≥0.5 away; nearest Water Cannon is at least one bay (1 unit) or on opposite side (2+ units) away, preventing accidental heating. Do not place torches on the same post as a Water Cannon.
         - Cannons remain OFF (no control activation) during evaluation.
      5) Side truss bracing (visual lattice + rigidity):
         - Longitudinal top-chord ties: Along each parapet, connect adjacent top posts with Braces—one brace between the north face of a top post at y and the south face of the next top post at y+1 (all y pairs). These braces align with the deck length, visually “stringing” the top chord as a continuous line.
         - Diagonal side panels: For each side and for each panel (between y and y+1), add a diagonal Brace from the outer face of the east-edge deck block at y (or west-edge deck block for that side) up to the inner face of the top post at y+1 on the same side, alternating orientation every other panel to create a repeating lattice (Warren) pattern. Braces have no physical volume, so they visually intersect without collision issues.
      6) Cross-bracing across width (to stabilize torsion):
         - At y = −4, 0, and +4, connect the inner faces of the east and west top posts across the deck with Braces (three cross-ties). Optionally add another set connecting east–west inner faces of edge deck blocks at the same y to form X-braces across the deck width at these bays.
      7) Central pylon and decorative winch suspenders:
         - Build a small pylon at the center: Starting from the top face of the center deck block at (x=0, y=0), stack 3 Small Wooden Blocks vertically (to z=+1, +2, +3). Attach one Small Wooden Block to the east face (x=+1, z≈+3) and one to the west face (x=−1, z≈+3) to form a short cross-beam.
         - Suspenders via Winches: For added visual complexity, connect Winches from the north/south faces of the upper pylon block(s) and the cross-beam blocks down to the top faces of select center-row deck blocks at y=±2, ±4, ±6. Use different faces (north, south, east, west) of the pylon and cross-beam to anchor multiple winches without face conflicts. The cables will angle outward and downward, suggesting catenaries. Because winches connect faces and have no collision volume, they won’t interfere with other parts.
    </assembly>
    <motion_control>
      - Powered Wheels: Do not assign control keys or leave unbound; they remain static ornamental medallions. The wheels self-brake when not driven.
      - Water Cannons: Leave unbound (or ensure no control keys are pressed). Cannons must remain OFF to avoid recoil. They are decorative only.
      - Torches: Always lit by nature; oriented outward so their 0.3-radius heating spheres remain in free air. Avoid placing torches behind or near water cannons to prevent accidental steam mode. Current alternating placement guarantees clearance.
      - No propulsion or locomotion is desired. The structure is passive; it will be gently placed over the gap and will settle onto both sides by gravity.
    </motion_control>
  </overall_structure>

  <sub_structures>
    <sub_structure_1>
      <name>Ornate Through-Truss Bridge (Single-Piece, 3×13 Deck)</name>
      <description>
        A single, rigid bridge assembly spanning 13 units north–south and 3 units east–west, with two-tier parapets, alternating sculptural Water Cannons and Torch lanterns, underhung Powered Wheel medallions, rich side truss bracing, cross-bracing, and a central pylon with winch suspenders. It is designed to be lowered into place to rest on terrain edges without anchors, while keeping the deck well above the gap bottom.
      </description>
      <components>
        - Default stone base block (1×1×1): 1 (mandatory, embedded at deck center).
        - Small Wooden Block (1×1×1): approximately 39 (deck) + 6 (end feet) + 52 (parapet posts) + 5 (central pylon and cross-beam) + 12 (wheel medallion bases) ≈ 114 total (allow ±10 for small cleats/extra anchors if needed).
        - Powered Wheel (2×2×0.5): 12 (odd y bays on both sides, underhung on medallion bases).
        - Water Cannon (1×1.75×1): 12 (alternating bays on parapet top posts: even y on east, odd y on west).
        - Torch (1.5×0.5×0.5): 12 (alternating opposite to cannons).
        - Brace: 30–40 (longitudinal top-chord ties along both sides, diagonal side-panel lattice, three east–west cross-ties, optional deck cross X-braces).
        - Winch: 6–10 (decorative suspenders from the central pylon/cross-beam down to deck blocks at y=±2, ±4, ±6; optionally to inner faces of parapet posts).
      </components>
      <assembly>
        - Coordinate frame: x east–west, y north–south (gap along y), z up. Place the center of the default stone at (0, 0, 0).
        1) Deck:
           - Center row (x=0): Attach 6 Small Wooden Blocks to the north face and 6 to the south face of the stone, forming y ∈ {−6,…,+6}.
           - Edge rows: For each center-row block at (0, y, 0), attach one Small Wooden Block on its east face (to x=+1) and one on its west face (to x=−1), producing a 3×13 deck grid.
           - End feet: At y=±6, attach one Small Wooden Block to the bottom faces of each x ∈ {−1, 0, +1} deck block (six total feet).
        2) Parapets:
           - For each edge deck block (x=±1, all y), attach a Small Wooden Block to its top face (z=+1), then another Small Wooden Block atop that (z=+2) to form 2-high posts.
        3) Underhung Powered Wheels:
           - For y odd (±1, ±3, ±5):
             • East side: attach a Small Wooden Block to the bottom face of the east-edge deck block (x=+1, z=−1) as a medallion base, then attach a Powered Wheel to that base’s east face; wheel axis along +x, disc vertical in YZ.
             • West side: mirror as above on x=−1, wheel attached to the west face of the base.
        4) Alternating Cannons and Torches (top of posts):
           - Even y: attach Water Cannons to the outer face of east top posts (x=+1, z≈+2), oriented upward and inward; attach Torches to the outer face of west top posts (x=−1, z≈+2), oriented upward and outward.
           - Odd y: reverse—Cannons on west top posts, Torches on east top posts.
           - Confirm that Torch flame sphere centers ~0.5 outboard and 1 unit above the mount, ensuring ≥0.5 clearance to any wood or cannon (well >0.3 heating radius). 
        5) Side truss Braces:
           - Along each parapet, connect adjacent top posts (north face of y to south face of y+1) to create a continuous top tie line with Braces.
           - For each panel, add one diagonal Brace from the outer face of the edge deck block at y to the inner face of the top post at y+1 on the same side. Alternate slants (/, \, /, \…) along the length for a Warren-truss motif.
        6) Cross-bracing:
           - At y = −4, 0, +4, connect east–west inner faces of top posts with Braces. Optionally add a second brace at the same y connecting east–west inner faces of edge deck blocks to form X-braced portals across the deck width.
        7) Central pylon and Winches:
           - Build a 3-high pylon on the top face of the center deck block at (0,0), then attach a cross-beam block to the pylon’s east and west faces at the top. 
           - Attach Winches from pylon/cross-beam faces (north, south, east, west) down to the top faces of center-row deck blocks at y=±2, ±4, ±6 (and/or to the inner faces of parapet posts at those y’s), forming decorative angled suspenders. Distribute attachments so no single face is overused; if necessary, add a small wooden “cleat” block to gain an extra free face for winch anchoring.
      </assembly>
      <motion_control>
        - Do not apply any driving input to Powered Wheels.
        - Do not fire Water Cannons (keep controls unbound or unpressed).
        - Torches remain lit and outward-facing; ensure no backward-facing steam jets exist (none are used).
        - The structure is passive; placement over the gap and gentle settling are sufficient for final position.
      </motion_control>
      <function>
        - Spans the 10-unit north–south gap via a 13-unit-long, rigid 3-wide deck.
        - Maintains the deck above the bottom of the gap by resting on the two edges; the wide base and triangulated parapets resist rotation and keep the walking surface high.
        - Maximizes visual and block diversity using all available block types in an integrated, ornate composition.
        - Braces and Winches add both ornamental complexity and supplemental rigidity, while the Water Cannons and Powered Wheels serve as bold visual elements without mechanical activation.
      </function>
      <design_requirements>
        - The bridge must remain a single sub-structure; do not separate any parts after assembly.
        - Ensure the deck length remains 13 units to guarantee ≥10 units clear span with end overhangs.
        - Keep Torches’ heating spheres in free air: mount on outer faces of top posts; maintain ≥0.5-unit clearance to wood and Water Cannons (heating radius is 0.3).
        - Keep Water Cannons OFF. Use alternating placement to avoid proximity to torches on the same post.
        - Place Powered Wheels only on underhung medallion bases (beneath edge deck blocks) to avoid interference with posts and walking surface.
        - Use connector Braces and Winches only between free, attachable faces; add small wooden “cleat” blocks if additional free faces are required for multiple connectors at the same node.
        - Positions may be micro‑adjusted in later stages to resolve conflicts based on actual build execution.
      </design_requirements>
    </sub_structure_1>
  </sub_structures>
</building_plan>

title: drafter message
summary: Ornate Through-Truss Bridge (Single-Piece, 3×13 Deck) Global axes: x east, y north, z up. Default base stone at (0, 0, 0); all placements refer to compass faces and z stacking from that origin. Position adjustment notes…
body:
Ornate Through-Truss Bridge (Single-Piece, 3×13 Deck)
Global axes: x east, y north, z up. Default base stone at (0, 0, 0); all placements refer to compass faces and z stacking from that origin.

Position adjustment notes (design-fit and safety)
- Fixtures at y=0 omitted: We intentionally leave the parapet top posts at y=0 free of Water Cannon/Torch to (a) clear the central pylon headroom and (b) meet the specified 12 Cannons and 12 Torches total.
- Diagonal side braces every other bay: To stay within 30–40 braces total while keeping a clean Warren motif, diagonals are installed only on even-start bays (y from −6 to +5 stepping by 2) on each side. This yields 12 diagonals total (6 per side). All longitudinal top-chord ties are retained (24). Three east–west cross ties installed at y=−4, 0, +4. Total braces = 39.
- Torch clearance: Torches mounted on outer faces with flame centers 0.5 unit outboard and 1 unit above mount. Heating sphere radius 0.3 has >0.5 clearance to all wood and cannons by alternating sides and skipping the y=0 pair.

Deck (3×13 grid, z=0; the base stone occupies the center of the center row)
- Block 0 - Default Starting Block - Stone base at deck center (0,0,0). Reference anchor for the whole build.

Center row x=0 (Small Wooden Blocks, z=0)
- Block 1 - Small Wooden Block - Center row (0, +1, 0) - north face of Block 0
- Block 2 - Small Wooden Block - (0, +2, 0) - north face of Block 1
- Block 3 - Small Wooden Block - (0, +3, 0) - north face of Block 2
- Block 4 - Small Wooden Block - (0, +4, 0) - north face of Block 3
- Block 5 - Small Wooden Block - (0, +5, 0) - north face of Block 4
- Block 6 - Small Wooden Block - (0, +6, 0) - north face of Block 5
- Block 7 - Small Wooden Block - (0, −1, 0) - south face of Block 0
- Block 8 - Small Wooden Block - (0, −2, 0) - south face of Block 7
- Block 9 - Small Wooden Block - (0, −3, 0) - south face of Block 8
- Block 10 - Small Wooden Block - (0, −4, 0) - south face of Block 9
- Block 11 - Small Wooden Block - (0, −5, 0) - south face of Block 10
- Block 12 - Small Wooden Block - (0, −6, 0) - south face of Block 11

Edge rows x=+1 (east) and x=−1 (west), z=0; one attached to each center-row block and to Block 0
- Block 13 - Small Wooden Block - East edge ( +1, −6, 0 ) - east face of Block 12
- Block 14 - Small Wooden Block - West edge ( −1, −6, 0 ) - west face of Block 12
- Block 15 - Small Wooden Block - East edge ( +1, −5, 0 ) - east face of Block 11
- Block 16 - Small Wooden Block - West edge ( −1, −5, 0 ) - west face of Block 11
- Block 17 - Small Wooden Block - East edge ( +1, −4, 0 ) - east face of Block 10
- Block 18 - Small Wooden Block - West edge ( −1, −4, 0 ) - west face of Block 10
- Block 19 - Small Wooden Block - East edge ( +1, −3, 0 ) - east face of Block 9
- Block 20 - Small Wooden Block - West edge ( −1, −3, 0 ) - west face of Block 9
- Block 21 - Small Wooden Block - East edge ( +1, −2, 0 ) - east face of Block 8
- Block 22 - Small Wooden Block - West edge ( −1, −2, 0 ) - west face of Block 8
- Block 23 - Small Wooden Block - East edge ( +1, −1, 0 ) - east face of Block 7
- Block 24 - Small Wooden Block - West edge ( −1, −1, 0 ) - west face of Block 7
- Block 25 - Small Wooden Block - East edge ( +1, 0, 0 ) - east face of Block 0
- Block 26 - Small Wooden Block - West edge ( −1, 0, 0 ) - west face of Block 0
- Block 27 - Small Wooden Block - East edge ( +1, +1, 0 ) - east face of Block 1
- Block 28 - Small Wooden Block - West edge ( −1, +1, 0 ) - west face of Block 1
- Block 29 - Small Wooden Block - East edge ( +1, +2, 0 ) - east face of Block 2
- Block 30 - Small Wooden Block - West edge ( −1, +2, 0 ) - west face of Block 2
- Block 31 - Small Wooden Block - East edge ( +1, +3, 0 ) - east face of Block 3
- Block 32 - Small Wooden Block - West edge ( −1, +3, 0 ) - west face of Block 3
- Block 33 - Small Wooden Block - East edge ( +1, +4, 0 ) - east face of Block 4
- Block 34 - Small Wooden Block - West edge ( −1, +4, 0 ) - west face of Block 4
- Block 35 - Small Wooden Block - East edge ( +1, +5, 0 ) - east face of Block 5
- Block 36 - Small Wooden Block - West edge ( −1, +5, 0 ) - west face of Block 5
- Block 37 - Small Wooden Block - East edge ( +1, +6, 0 ) - east face of Block 6

Alternating Water Cannons and Torches on parapet top posts (outer face of z=+2 posts; skip y=0)

Odd y (−5, −3, −1, +1, +3, +5): Cannons on west outer face; Torches on east outer face.
Mount geometry reminders:
- Water Cannon: attach to outer vertical face; orient outlet upward and slightly inward toward deck centerline; keep controls unbound/off. Recoil vector points downward/upward mix; structure is massive; no firing planned.
- Torch: attach to outer vertical face; oriented upward and outward. Heating sphere center ~0.5 outboard, 1.0 above mount; radius 0.3; clear of wood/cannons by ≥0.5.

Water Cannons (12 total; function set: OFF)
- Block 121 - Water Cannon - y=−6 on East post - outer (east) face of Block 46 - Shoots upward and inward toward deck; OFF/not bound
- Block 122 - Water Cannon - y=−5 on West post - outer (west) face of Block 52 - Shoots upward and inward; OFF
- Block 123 - Water Cannon - y=−4 on East post - outer (east) face of Block 54 - Shoots upward and inward; OFF
- Block 124 - Water Cannon - y=−3 on West post - outer (west) face of Block 60 - Shoots upward and inward; OFF
- Block 125 - Water Cannon - y=−2 on East post - outer (east) face of Block 62 - Shoots upward and inward; OFF
- Block 126 - Water Cannon - y=−1 on West post - outer (west) face of Block 68 - Shoots upward and inward; OFF

- Block 128 - Water Cannon - y=+2 on East post - outer (east) face of Block 78 - Shoots upward and inward; OFF

Torches (12 total; flames outward/up; keep clear of cannons by alternating sides; torches have no attachable faces)

- Block 134 - Torch - y=−5 on East post - outer (east) face of Block 50 - Flame upward/outward
- Block 135 - Torch - y=−4 on West post - outer (west) face of Block 56 - Flame upward/outward

- Block 137 - Torch - y=−2 on West post - outer (west) face of Block 64 - Flame upward/outward

Central pylon and cross-beam (over (0,0), z=+1 to +3)

Winches (6 total; decorative suspenders from pylon/cross-beam to deck top faces)
Note: Winches connect two attachable faces; listed with their two endpoints.
- Block 150 - Winch - From north face of Block 147 (pylon top) to top face of Block 6 (deck center at y=+6)
- Block 151 - Winch - From south face of Block 147 (pylon top) to top face of Block 12 (deck center at y=−6)
- Block 152 - Winch - From north face of Block 148 (east arm) to top face of Block 2 (deck center at y=+2)
- Block 153 - Winch - From south face of Block 149 (west arm) to top face of Block 8 (deck center at y=−2)
- Block 154 - Winch - From north face of Block 149 (west arm) to top face of Block 4 (deck center at y=+4)
- Block 155 - Winch - From south face of Block 148 (east arm) to top face of Block 10 (deck center at y=−4)

Side truss Braces (39 total)
Top-chord ties (24): connect adjacent parapet top posts along each side: north face of post at y to south face of post at y+1, z=+2. East side uses Blocks [46,50,54,58,62,66,70,74,78,82,86,90,94]; West side uses Blocks [48,52,56,60,64,68,72,76,80,84,88,92,96]. We connect consecutive pairs (−6→−5, … , +5→+6) per side.
- Block 156..167 - Brace - East side top-chord ties: for each k in { (46–50), (50–54), (54–58), (58–62), (62–66), (66–70), (70–74), (74–78), (78–82), (82–86), (86–90), (90–94) } connect north face of first to south face of second.
- Block 168..179 - Brace - West side top-chord ties: for each k in { (48–52), (52–56), (56–60), (60–64), (64–68), (68–72), (72–76), (76–80), (80–84), (84–88), (88–92), (92–96) } connect north face of first to south face of second.

Diagonal side-panel lattice (12): even-start bays only (y=−6, −4, −2, 0, +2, +4) to inner face of next post at y+1, forming alternating slants by side.
East side diagonals (start at Blocks 13,17,21,25,29,33 to inner face of Blocks 50,54,58,74,78,82 respectively)
- Block 180 - Brace - East diagonal y=−6→−5: from outer face of Block 13 to inner (west) face of Block 50
- Block 181 - Brace - East diagonal y=−4→−3: from outer face of Block 17 to inner face of Block 58
- Block 182 - Brace - East diagonal y=−2→−1: from outer face of Block 21 to inner face of Block 66
- Block 183 - Brace - East diagonal y=0→+1: from outer face of Block 25 to inner face of Block 74
- Block 184 - Brace - East diagonal y=+2→+3: from outer face of Block 29 to inner face of Block 82
- Block 185 - Brace - East diagonal y=+4→+5: from outer face of Block 33 to inner face of Block 90

West side diagonals (start at Blocks 14,18,22,26,30,34 to inner face of Blocks 52,56,60,76,80,84 respectively) mirrored slant
- Block 186 - Brace - West diagonal y=−6→−5: from outer face of Block 14 to inner (east) face of Block 52
- Block 187 - Brace - West diagonal y=−4→−3: from outer face of Block 18 to inner face of Block 60
- Block 188 - Brace - West diagonal y=−2→−1: from outer face of Block 22 to inner face of Block 68
- Block 189 - Brace - West diagonal y=0→+1: from outer face of Block 26 to inner face of Block 76
- Block 190 - Brace - West diagonal y=+2→+3: from outer face of Block 30 to inner face of Block 84
- Block 191 - Brace - West diagonal y=+4→+5: from outer face of Block 34 to inner face of Block 92

Cross-bracing at y=−4, 0, +4 across width (3)
- Block 192 - Brace - Portal tie at y=−4: connect inner face of East top post Block 54 to inner face of West top post Block 56
- Block 193 - Brace - Portal tie at y=0: connect inner face of East top post Block 70 to inner face of West top post Block 72
- Block 194 - Brace - Portal tie at y=+4: connect inner face of East top post Block 86 to inner face of West top post Block 88

Motion and safety configuration
- Powered Wheels (Blocks 109–120): Decorative only. Do not bind to any input; leave at default 100 rpm idle with auto-brake. Orientation set with axis along ±x; discs vertical in YZ; underhung on medallion bases below edge deck blocks at odd y to avoid deck interference.
- Water Cannons (Blocks 121–132): Keep OFF/unpressed. Oriented upward and slightly inward from the outer face to avoid torches. No steam mode expected (no intentional heating); recoil irrelevant since not firing.
- Torches (Blocks 133–144): Always lit, flames upward/outward; heating spheres centered ~0.5 outboard and 1.0 above post faces; ≥0.5 clearance from any wood and cannons ensured via side alternation and y=0 skip.

Function checks
- Deck spans 13 units north–south (y=−6..+6) and 3 units east–west (x=−1,0,+1). End feet at y=±6 provide bearing pads on terrain.
- Triangulated parapets and cross-ties stiffen the single-piece assembly. Central pylon with winch suspenders adds ornamental complexity and perceived support.
- All connectors (Braces/Winches) attach between free faces only; where multiple ties meet, we used distinct faces (outer/inner/north/south) of posts and deck blocks; no cleats required in this layout.
- All torches and cannons mounted on opposite sides by y parity with y=0 skipped, guaranteeing heating clearance and avoiding any steam backwash interaction.

If any build-time interference appears (e.g., due to tolerances around cannons’ peanut waist vs. torch flame centerlines), shift the cannon orientation a few degrees more inward or outward at that bay, or swap the torch/cannon assignment on that single bay while maintaining the even/odd global pattern.

title: draft_reviewer message
summary: Reviewer summary: I have checked the proposed “Ornate Through‑Truss Bridge (Single‑Piece, 3×13 Deck)” against the structural, functional, and feasibility requirements. The build is logically organized, uses the mandator…
body:
Reviewer summary: I have checked the proposed “Ornate Through‑Truss Bridge (Single‑Piece, 3×13 Deck)” against the structural, functional, and feasibility requirements. The build is logically organized, uses the mandatory base, and stays as a single sub‑structure. I verify collision‑free placement via systematic category checks (deck grid, feet, parapets, underhung wheel assemblies, cannons, torches, pylon, winches, and braces). Where the same pattern repeats (e.g., the 3×13 deck and two‑high parapet posts), I verify one full pattern and show that all instances follow by symmetry. I also identify one minor documentation inconsistency on a torch flame center and one orientation constraint that must be respected for the water cannons to avoid any overlap. With those clarifications, the structure passes the checks.

Conventions used in this review
- Global axes: x east, y north, z up. All block dimensions are axis‑aligned bounding boxes unless otherwise stated by the part’s orientation.
- Base block (stone) center at (0, 0, 0). Small Wooden Block dimensions: 1×1×1; half‑extent 0.5 along each axis.
- Distance/clearance check: To avoid overlap between two solids, the center‑to‑center projection along each axis must be ≥ the sum of the corresponding half‑extents along that axis. For face‑to‑face contact along one axis with same y, z (or x, z) and equal sizes, centers are 1 unit apart along that axis.
- Connectors (Braces, Winches) have no volume; only endpoint face availability is validated.
- Water Cannon: shape 1×1.75×1; when attached to a vertical face, its center is 0.5 outward from the face plane. Orientation sets the 1.75‑unit length axis. Cross‑section half‑extent (perpendicular to its length) is 0.5.
- Torch: center is 0.5 outward from the mounting face; the flame center is 1.0 above the torch center along the shaft direction. Heating sphere radius = 0.3.
- Powered Wheel: shape 2×2×0.5. Attached to a side face; axis perpendicular to that face; wheel disc lies in the face plane. For non‑overlap and “flush” attachment, I place the wheel center 0.25 beyond the face plane (so its inner face is flush with the mounting face), giving x‑offset 0.25 for east/west mounts (this is the physically plausible standoff given the 0.5 thickness; see Section 3 below).

1) Deck (3×13 grid) and end feet
- Centers as listed by the drafter:
  • Center row x=0: (0, y, 0), y ∈ {−6…+6}, including base (0,0,0). Each attached to the north or south face of its neighbor; center‑to‑center distance along y = 1.
  • Edge rows x=±1: (±1, y, 0), y ∈ {−6…+6}, each attached to the east/west face of the center row counterpart; Δx = 1 to its center‑row neighbor.
  • End feet: (x, ±6, −1) for x ∈ {−1, 0, +1}, each attached to bottom face of the deck block above; Δz = 1 to its supported deck block.

- Neighbor distances and overlap checks (pattern applies to all deck blocks):
  • Face‑adjacent blocks: Δ = 1 along the attachment axis; half‑extent sum = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1 → face contact, no overlap.
  • Edge diagonals (e.g., a center row block and its adjacent in x and y): center separations are √(1^2 + 1^2) = √2 > 1; min sum along any axis is at most 1, so no overlap.
  • End feet to neighboring deck blocks at the same y: Δz = 1; no overlap. To deck blocks at adjacent y: centers differ by at least √(1^2 + 1^2) ≥ √2; no overlap.
- Result: 3×13 deck and feet are collision‑free and fully attached to the base chain centered at (0,0,0).

2) Parapet posts (two‑high on x=±1 edges, z=+1 and z=+2)
- Centers exactly as listed by the drafter: for each edge deck block (±1, y, 0), add (±1, y, +1) and (±1, y, +2).
- Neighbor distances:
  • Each post P1 at z=+1 is Δz = 1 from its deck block; P2 at z=+2 is Δz = 1 from P1 below → face contact, no overlap.
  • Posts at same x on adjacent y are Δy = 1 between centers; disjoint volumes (only Braces connect them, which have no volume).
- Result: All parapet posts are collision‑free and properly attached.

3) Underhung medallion bases and Powered Wheels (y odd: ±1, ±3, ±5)
- Medallion base blocks: centers (±1, y, −1), attached to bottom faces of edge deck blocks at those y. Neighbor distances identical to end feet case: Δz = 1 to the deck block above, no overlap.
- Powered Wheel placement and orientation:
  • Wheels attach to the east face of east bases and west face of west bases.
  • Mount face planes: east base face at x = +1.5, west base face at x = −1.5.
  • Wheel center offsets: chosen at 0.25 beyond the face plane to make the 0.5 thickness sit flush on the mounting face.
    – East wheels: center (x, y, z) = (+1.75, y, −1) for y ∈ {−5, −3, −1, +1, +3, +5}.
    – West wheels: center = (−1.75, y, −1) for the same y set.
  • Axis and disc orientation: axis along ±x (perpendicular to mounting face); wheel disc lies in the YZ plane (vertical), as specified. No control bound; decorative only.
- Neighbor distances:
  • Wheel to its medallion base: Δx = 0.75 (1.75 − 1.0 for east), half‑extent sum along x = 0.25 (wheel) + 0.5 (cube) = 0.75 → exact face contact (inner wheel face flush to base face), no overlap.
  • Wheel to edge deck block above: deck x‑range [0.5, 1.5] (east), wheel x‑range [1.5, 2.0]; their x‑ranges meet at x=1.5 but do not overlap. Along z, the wheel extends z ∈ [−2, 0], the deck block spans z ∈ [−0.5, +0.5]; at x=1.5 they share only a boundary in x and do not interpenetrate → no overlap.
  • Wheels at adjacent y are ≥ 2 units apart center‑to‑center at worst (since they are at y separated by 2), with half‑extents along y at most 1 (wheel diameter) → no overlap.
- Result: underhung wheel assemblies are consistent with the part rules and collision‑free.

4) Water Cannons and Torches on parapet top posts (skip y=0)
Water Cannons (OFF; mounted on outer faces of z=+2 posts; alternating per parity)
- Centers:
  • East side even y ∈ {−6, −4, −2, +2, +4, +6}: mount to outer (east) face of post at (1, y, 2). Face plane x=+1.5 → cannon center at (2.0, y, 2.0).
  • West side odd y ∈ {−5, −3, −1, +1, +3, +5}: outer (west) face of post at (−1, y, 2). Face plane x=−1.5 → cannon center at (−2.0, y, 2.0).
- Orientation and no‑overlap constraint:
  • Axis is the 1.75‑unit length direction “upward and slightly inward.” Let the inward tilt from vertical be φ, with the inward x‑component magnitude |d_x| = sinφ and vertical component d_z = cosφ (no y component as per the intent).
  • To prevent the inward half of the body from crossing the mounting post’s outer face (x=±1.5), require the furthest inward centerline point x_min to satisfy x_min ≥ ±1.5. With center at ±2.0 and half‑length 0.875, this yields |d_x| ≤ 0.5714 → φ ≤ 34.8°. If you keep φ ≤ 30°, the cannon cannot intersect the post or deck.
- Neighbor distances:
  • Cannon to its mounting post center: Δx = 1.0, sum of half‑extents along x = 0.5 (post) + 0.5 (cannon) = 1.0 → flush mount, no overlap.
  • Cannon to adjacent torch on same side at neighboring bay (Δy = 1): cannon half‑extent along y = 0.5; torch half‑extent along y = 0.25 → required Δy ≥ 0.75; actual Δy = 1 → 0.25 margin, no overlap.
  • Across the deck, opposite side devices are ≥ 4 units apart in x → no overlap.

Torches (always lit; mounted on outer faces; alternating opposite to cannons)
- Centers and flame centers (correcting one misreported example):
  • East side odd y: torch center (2.0, y, 2.0); flame center at (2.0, y, 3.0).
  • West side even y: torch center (−2.0, y, 2.0); flame center at (−2.0, y, 3.0).
  • Note: Drafter’s Block 133 stated flame center “~[−1.5, −6, 3]”; by the torch definition it should be (−2.0, −6, 3.0) because the torch center is 0.5 beyond the face plane (x=−1.5) → center at x=−2.0.
- Neighbor distances and heating clearance:
  • To own post: nearest point is the top‑outer edge at (±1.5, y, 2.5); distance from flame center to that edge ≈ √((0.5)^2 + (0.5)^2) = 0.707 > 0.3 (heating radius). Sphere boundary clearance = 0.707 − 0.3 ≈ 0.407. If the intended “≥0.5 clearance” means center‑to‑object distance, 0.707 ≥ 0.5 is satisfied; if it means boundary‑to‑object gap ≥ 0.5, this is slightly under at ≈ 0.407. Given the original requirement’s wording is ambiguous, please confirm the metric; otherwise, the physical heating radius condition (0.3) is safely met.
  • To nearest adjacent water cannon at the neighboring bay on the same side: centers differ by Δy = 1 and Δz = 1; shortest center‑to‑solid distance ≈ √((1−0.5)^2 + (1−0.5)^2) = 0.707; sphere boundary clearance ≈ 0.707 − 0.3 = 0.407 (>0), again safe versus heating, but slightly below a 0.5 boundary gap if that stricter interpretation is enforced.
  • To any opposite side devices: ≥ 4 units in x; trivially safe.
- Result: mounting positions and alternating pattern avoid interference. Heating spheres are in free air and clear of solids by more than their 0.3 radius. If a 0.5 boundary‑gap is strictly required, a small outward cant of the torch head or reducing cannon inward tilt further will increase the minimum gap above 0.5.

5) Central pylon and cross‑beam
- Centers per drafter:
  • Pylon: (0,0,1), (0,0,2), (0,0,3) stacked; each Δz = 1 from the one below → face contact, no overlap.
  • Cross‑beam arms: (±1, 0, 3) on east/west faces of top pylon block (0,0,3). These are face‑mounted; Δx = 1 to the pylon top, no overlap.
- Neighbor distances:
  • Cross‑beam arm (±1, 0, 3) to y=0 parapet posts at (±1, 0, 2): Δz = 1 → touching faces at z=2.5 plane; no overlap and permitted (they are parts of the same connected assembly through the deck and pylon).

6) Winches (no volume)
- Attachments as listed use free faces:
  • Pylon top (0,0,3): north and south faces used by Blocks 150, 151; east and west faces are used by cross‑beam arms, leaving no conflict.
  • Cross‑beam arms: north face of Block 148 and south face of Block 148 used once; north face of Block 149 and south face of Block 149 used once — no face overuse.
  • Deck top faces at y=±2, ±4, ±6 (center row): unused by posts; available for winch anchors; each used once.
- Result: All winch endpoints are on free, attachable faces; no conflicts.

7) Braces (no volume)
- Longitudinal top‑chord ties (24): connect north face of post at y to south face at y+1 on each side (z=+2). Faces are free (outer faces used by devices, inner faces reserved for diagonals or portals; north/south faces dedicated here). No face overuse on any single face.
- Diagonal side‑panel lattice (12): even‑start bays only (y = −6, −4, −2, 0, +2, +4).
  • East side: from outer face of edge deck block at y to inner face (west) of top post at y+1. These inner faces are not used by longitudinal ties; outer deck faces are free. No conflicts.
  • West side: symmetric, using inner (east) faces of posts at y+1 and outer faces of edge deck blocks at y.
- Cross‑bracing portals at y=−4, 0, +4 (3): connect inner faces of east/west top posts at those y. Those specific inner faces are not used by diagonals at the same y. No conflicts.

8) Functional validation
- Motion:
  • Powered Wheels: Axis along ±x, disc vertical in YZ; mounted to side faces — consistent with the rules (axis perpendicular to attached face, disc parallel to face). No inputs bound, so no active motion and auto‑brake keeps them static.
  • Water Cannons: OFF; recoil irrelevant. Orientation “upward and slightly inward” is functionally acceptable provided the inward tilt φ ≤ 34.8° (recommend φ ≤ 30°) to avoid any geometric intrusion into the mounting post or deck volume.
  • Torches: lit, outward/upward; flame spheres located at (±2, y, 3) with radius 0.3; free of solids. No steam cannons behind them, so no extinguishing issues.
- Mounting and alignment:
  • All moving/functional components mount on appropriate faces: wheels on medallion bases’ side faces; cannons on top‑post outer faces; torches on top‑post outer faces. Axes/orientations are consistent with part rules.

9) Physical feasibility
- The 3×13×3 deck grid with two‑high parapets, diagonals, and cross‑ties forms a rigid trussed frame. The end feet at y=±6 provide bearing pads; the deck length 13 ≥ 10 clear span requirement is satisfied.
- The assembly remains a single sub‑structure: all blocks are face‑attached chains originating from the base; connectors link already existing blocks; nothing is detached.

10) Specific computed centers and neighbor checks for special parts
- Powered Wheels (east side y=−5…+5 odd): centers (1.75, y, −1); nearest neighbor medallion base (1, y, −1) at Δx = 0.75 (half‑sum 0.75) → flush, no overlap; to edge deck block (1, y, 0), x‑ranges only touch at x=1.5; no overlap.
- Water Cannons: centers at (2.0, y, 2.0) on east even y; (−2.0, y, 2.0) on west odd y. To the mounting post center (±1, y, 2): Δx = 1 equals half‑sum 1 → flush, no overlap. To adjacent torch on same side at neighboring y: Δy = 1 > 0.75 (half‑sum in y), no overlap. Tilt constraint φ ≤ 34.8° ensures no intrusion into x ≤ ±1.5.
- Torches: centers at (2.0, y, 2.0) for east odd y and (−2.0, y, 2.0) for west even y; flame centers at (±2.0, y, 3.0). To nearest wood (outer top‑post edge): center‑to‑corner distance ≈ 0.707; sphere boundary clearance ≈ 0.407 (> 0.0). To nearest cannon: center‑to‑body distance ≈ 0.707; boundary clearance ≈ 0.407 (> 0.0). If a 0.5 boundary gap is mandated, cant the torch head slightly outboard or further reduce cannon inward tilt (e.g., φ ≤ 20°) to increase the gap; as built, the heating radius requirement is satisfied and ignition risk is negligible.

11) Minor correction noted
- Drafter’s Block 133 flame center “~[−1.5, −6, 3]” should be (−2.0, −6, 3.0) per the Torch attachment rule (0.5 outward from the face plane x=−1.5). This documentation fix does not affect the collision checks above.

Conclusion
- Structural integrity and collision‑free placement: Pass, with the explicit cannon inward tilt constraint φ ≤ 34.8° (recommended ≤ 30°) to guarantee no geometric intrusion into posts; and the corrected torch flame center reporting.
- Functional implementation: Pass (wheels static; cannons OFF; torches lit and outward; no backward jets; proper orientations).
- Physical feasibility: Pass (13‑unit span, triangulated parapets, end feet supports; single sub‑structure).

Approval
The blueprint, with the stated cannon tilt constraint and the corrected torch flame center coordinates, meets the requirements. Please keep the cannons’ inward tilt ≤ 30° to maintain generous clearances and, if a ≥0.5 boundary gap to solids is strictly required for torch flame spheres, cant torches slightly further outward or reduce cannon inward tilt to achieve that margin; otherwise the heating radius safety is already satisfied.

TERMINATE

title: builder thought
summary: Tooling overview and how we’ll use them step by step I have the following tools available in this build simulation. Please note that every action adds exactly one block or modifies one existing block, and I will only ma…
body:
Tooling overview and how we’ll use them step by step

I have the following tools available in this build simulation. Please note that every action adds exactly one block or modifies one existing block, and I will only make one tool call per message so we can build safely and verify as we go. Also, the note argument on many tools is very important: I will use it to tag each block/connection with its intended function (e.g., “Center row (0,+1,0)” or “East top-chord y=−6→−5”) so we can reliably identify and audit the structure later.

Available tools
- functions.start
  - Purpose: Create the initial starting block and place it in the world with a given shift and rotation.
  - Inputs needed: init_shift [x,y,z], init_rotation [yaw,pitch,roll] (optional), note (what we’re building).
  - Typical use here: Place the default Stone base at (0,0,0) to serve as Block 0.
- functions.attach_block_to
  - Purpose: Attach a new block directly to a specific face of an existing block.
  - Available new_block types: "Powered Wheel", "Small Wooden Block", "Torch", "Water Cannon".
  - Inputs needed: base_block (ID of existing block), face (the labeled face to attach to, e.g., North/South/East/West/Top/Bottom—captions can be confirmed with get_block_details), new_block (type to attach), note (critical for identification).
  - Notes: Faces are oriented to the global axes (east +x, north +y, up +z). This is the primary tool to add most of the blocks in your blueprint.
- functions.connect_blocks
  - Purpose: Connect two existing blocks by a connector element.
  - Connectors: "Brace" or "Winch".
  - Inputs needed: block_a, face_a, block_b, face_b, connector, note.
  - Notes: The connection will fail if faces are too close or invalid. I will choose the exact faces per the blueprint (e.g., “north face of Block 46 to south face of Block 50”). This places one connector per call.
- functions.get_block_details
  - Purpose: Retrieve full position/rotation/face info of a block.
  - Inputs needed: block_id.
  - Notes: I’ll use this when we need to confirm face names, orientation for wheels/cannons/torches, or to troubleshoot clearances.
- functions.shift_block
  - Purpose: Slightly adjust a block’s position (range per axis −0.5 to +0.5) after attachment, useful for minor interference.
  - Inputs needed: block_id, shift_real [dx,dy,dz].
  - Notes: If minor interference occurs (e.g., a post ornament contacts a torch), we can apply a small offset as your notes allow, while preserving alternating patterns.
- functions.twist_block
  - Purpose: Twist a block around its rooted surface by a given angle (degrees).
  - Inputs needed: block_id, angle.
  - Notes: Helpful for orienting Powered Wheels (axis along ±x) and aiming Water Cannons slightly inward/upward, or pointing Torch flames outward/upward.
- functions.flip_spin
  - Purpose: Flip the spin direction of a block that spins (e.g., a Powered Wheel).
  - Inputs needed: block_id.
  - Notes: Decorative wheels will remain unbound; we’ll ensure their axes are correct, but spin direction flips aren’t necessary since they’re decorative only.
- functions.remove_block
  - Purpose: Remove a block if we need to correct a mistake.
  - Inputs needed: block_id.
- functions.reset
  - Purpose: Reset the entire build to empty.
  - Inputs needed: none.
- functions.get_machine_summary
  - Purpose: Get the latest state overview of the whole build (without face captions). I will always use this for a final check before we end the process and will remind you before doing so.

Build approach for your Ornate Through-Truss Bridge (Single-Piece, 3×13 Deck)
- We will:
  1) Place the starting stone base at (0,0,0) as Block 0.
  2) Lay the center deck row northward then southward (Blocks 1–12).
  3) Add edge deck rows at x=±1 (Blocks 13–38).
  4) Add bearing feet at y=±6 (Blocks 39–44).
  5) Build parapet posts two-high on edge deck blocks (Blocks 45–96), skipping y=0 fixtures as specified for headroom.
  6) Install underhung medallion bases (Blocks 97–108).
  7) Attach Powered Wheels (Blocks 109–120) with correct axis along ±x; no controls bound.
  8) Mount Water Cannons and Torches on alternating z=+2 posts, skipping y=0 per your spec (Blocks 121–144), adjusting orientation to be upward and slightly inward (cannons) and upward/outward (torches) with clearance.
  9) Build the central pylon and cross-beam (Blocks 145–149).
  10) Add decorative Winches (Blocks 150–155).
  11) Add Braces for top-chords, side-panel diagonals, and portals (Blocks 156–194).
- Throughout, I’ll use the note field to label each placed block/connector precisely, and will verify/adjust orientation with twist_block as required for torches and cannons to ensure clearance and the specified alternation.

If everything looks good, I’ll start by placing Block 0, the starting Stone base at (0,0,0).

Proceeding to create Block 0 now.

body:
Instruction 113 (orientation correction):
- Reorient Water Cannon ID 128 (mounted on the west face of Block 77 at y=+1, z=+2) so that it sprays upward and slightly inward toward the bridge center (+x). Specifically, rotate it so the outlet is above the inlet (pointing straight up if fine control is limited). Target coordinates after adjustment:
  - Inlet ≈ [−2.00, +1.00, +1.25]
  - Outlet ≈ [−2.00, +1.00, +3.00]
- Keep the cannon OFF with no control bindings and do not use steam mode.

Please execute this orientation adjustment and report the updated inlet/outlet coordinates and spray direction. If fine yaw control is supported, cant the outlet a few degrees toward +x; otherwise straight up is acceptable.

title: guidance message
summary: Step 1 — Correct torch orientation error for safety and spec compliance Issue found: Torch ID 139 (east face of Block 67 at y = −1, z = +2) is pointing straight down and its heat sphere is centered at [2.0, −1.0, 1.0],…
body:
Step 1 — Correct torch orientation error for safety and spec compliance
Issue found: Torch ID 139 (east face of Block 67 at y = −1, z = +2) is pointing straight down and its heat sphere is centered at [2.0, −1.0, 1.0], which violates the blueprint (flame must be upward/outward) and risks heating/igniting bridge wood.

Instruction:
- Reorient Torch ID 139 so that its shaft points straight up (vertical), with the horizontal support extending outward along +x (east). The heating sphere center should be approximately at [2.0, −1.0, 3.0] with radius 0.3.
- Do not change its attachment: keep it on the east (outer) face of Block 67 (Small Wooden Block at (+1, −1, +2)).

Notes:
- No other torches need reorientation at this time; all others (IDs 134–138) are already vertical up with outward supports.
- Powered Wheels (IDs 110–121) are correctly oriented: axes along ±x, discs in YZ, rolling directions north for east wheels and south for west wheels, auto-brake enabled, and no inputs bound.
- Water Cannons (IDs 122–133) still need “slightly inward” cant per the blueprint; we will adjust them bay-by-bay after this torch fix.

Please apply this reorientation and then provide an updated status readout for Torch 139 (orientation and heat sphere center), after which I will proceed to refine the water cannon aiming.

title: builder thought
summary: Tooling introduction and how I'll use them for you: - functions.start: Creates the first block of a machine with an initial position and rotation. It also accepts a “note” string. That note is very important: we use it…
body:
Tooling introduction and how I'll use them for you:
- functions.start: Creates the first block of a machine with an initial position and rotation. It also accepts a “note” string. That note is very important: we use it as a human-readable identifier for the machine or special roles, so please include clear intent there when starting new builds.
- functions.get_machine_summary: Retrieves the latest overall state of the machine. I will always run this before we completely finish a task as a final consistency check.
- functions.get_block_details: Returns detailed information (position, rotation, face/attachment data) for a specific block. I’ll use this to verify orientations or clearances when needed.
- functions.twist_block: Rotates a block by a specified angle (degrees) around its rooted/attachment face. This is ideal for fixing the pointing direction of pointer-like parts (e.g., torches, cannons, wheels) without changing their mount.
- functions.shift_block: Shifts a specific block by a small offset vector [x, y, z] (each component in [-0.5, 0.5]) to resolve minor interferences while keeping attachments intact.
- functions.flip_spin: Flips the spin direction of a spinning block if it has spin capability.

Note on “note” argument: Among these, functions.start provides a “note” field. Using that note as a specific function tag is powerful for identifying the block/machine’s purpose during reviews.

Step 1 plan: Correct Torch 139 orientation while keeping its attachment on the east (outer) face of Block 67. Since it is currently pointed straight down, a 180° twist around its rooted east face will flip it to point straight up while keeping its support projecting outward along +x. After adjusting, I will fetch the detailed status for Torch 139 to confirm its orientation and the heat sphere center (~[2.0, -1.0, 3.0], r=0.3).

Executing: Twist Torch ID 139 by +180° around its mount.

title: guidance message
summary: Step 2 — Reorient the first Water Cannon to “upward and slightly inward” per blueprint Target: Water Cannon ID 122 on the east face of Block 47 at y = −6, z = +2. Instruction: - Rotate the cannon so its outlet points pr…
body:
Step 2 — Reorient the first Water Cannon to “upward and slightly inward” per blueprint
Target: Water Cannon ID 122 on the east face of Block 47 at y = −6, z = +2.

Instruction:
- Rotate the cannon so its outlet points primarily upward with a small inward cant toward the bridge center (−x). Aim for roughly 10–15° inward from vertical. A suitable spray direction is approximately [-0.2, 0.0, 0.98].
- Do not change its mount point or position. Keep the cannon OFF and with no bindings.
- Verify clearances: ensure no part of the cannon enters any torch’s 0.3-radius heat sphere. With the inward cant, it should be farther from torches on the west side, so clearance should remain ≥0.5.

Please apply this adjustment and report back the updated orientation readout for Water Cannon 122 (e.g., “Pointing at <up and slightly inward>” and/or a direction vector), then I will proceed to reorient the remaining cannons bay-by-bay.

summary: Step 3 — Correct yaw on Water Cannon 122 to point inward (-x) Current status shows Cannon 122 yawed toward North with 78° pitch; we need it inward toward the bridge center (West, i.e., −x) with the same “slightly inward…
body:
Step 3 — Correct yaw on Water Cannon 122 to point inward (-x)
Current status shows Cannon 122 yawed toward North with 78° pitch; we need it inward toward the bridge center (West, i.e., −x) with the same “slightly inward” up-tilt.

Instruction:
- Rotate Water Cannon ID 122 about the vertical axis so its outlet points West with the same 78° pitch (≈12° inward from vertical). Target direction vector ≈[−0.2, 0.0, 0.98].
- Keep mount, position, and OFF state unchanged.
- After adjustment, the readout should show “Pointing at <West with ~78° pitch>” (or equivalent “up and slightly inward (−x)”), with negligible y component (|y| ≤ 0.02).

Please apply this yaw correction and report the updated orientation readout (direction and inlet/outlet coordinates) for Water Cannon 122. After it’s correct, I will proceed to reorient the remaining cannons bay-by-bay.

title: guidance message
summary: Step 3 (retry) — Set Water Cannon 122 to “up and slightly inward” with zero Y component to avoid the torch Reason for previous failure: The last attempt yawed the cannon toward North, giving it a non‑zero Y component an…
body:
Step 3 (retry) — Set Water Cannon 122 to “up and slightly inward” with zero Y component to avoid the torch

Reason for previous failure: The last attempt yawed the cannon toward North, giving it a non‑zero Y component and moving its outlet near the adjacent torch at y = −5 (Torch ID 135), causing an overlap.

Instruction:
- Reorient Water Cannon ID 122 so its aim has:
  - Inward cant toward bridge center along −x (West),
  - No north/south component (Y component = 0),
  - Approximately 10–15° inward from vertical (about 78–80° pitch).
- A suitable direction vector is approximately [−0.2, 0.0, 0.98]. The readout may display “Pointing at <West with ~78–80° pitch>”.
- Keep mount point and OFF state unchanged. Do not enable steam.

Clearance note:
- By constraining the Y component to zero, the cannon stays in the y = −6 plane and clears Torch 135 at y = −5 by ≥0.5. If the system still flags a collision during the rotation, reduce the inward cant slightly (e.g., 82–85° pitch) while keeping Y = 0, then fine‑tune back toward ~78–80° as space permits.

Please apply this orientation and provide the updated readout for Water Cannon 122 (direction and inlet/outlet coordinates). After confirming this one is correct, I will proceed to reorient the remaining cannons bay-by-bay.

title: guidance message
summary: Step 3 — Set Water Cannon 122 to “upward and slightly inward” with zero Y to avoid torch interference Reasoning: Yawing while the cannon is perfectly vertical is a no-op in this engine, and a prior attempt introduced a…
body:
Step 3 — Set Water Cannon 122 to “upward and slightly inward” with zero Y to avoid torch interference

Reasoning: Yawing while the cannon is perfectly vertical is a no-op in this engine, and a prior attempt introduced a northward component that conflicted with the adjacent torch at y = −5. We’ll set a small inward cant toward −x with no y component.

Instruction:
- Reorient Water Cannon ID 122 (east face of Block 47 at y = −6, z = +2) so its spray direction vector is approximately [−0.10, 0.00, 0.995], i.e., West with a very slight inward tilt (about 84–85° pitch from horizon, ~5–6° from vertical). Keep the cannon OFF and on the same mount.
- This keeps the entire cannon within the y = −6 plane, ensuring clearance from Torch 135 at y = −5 while fulfilling the “upward and slightly inward” requirement.

Please apply this orientation and provide the updated readout for Water Cannon 122 (direction and inlet/outlet coordinates). After confirming this is correct, we will proceed bay-by-bay for the remaining cannons.

title: guidance message
summary: Step 3 (retry) — Snap Water Cannon 122 to “up and slightly inward” with zero north/south component Goal: For Cannon 122 (east face of Block 47 at y = −6, z = +2), set a slight inward cant toward the bridge center (−x, W…
body:
Step 3 (retry) — Snap Water Cannon 122 to “up and slightly inward” with zero north/south component

Goal: For Cannon 122 (east face of Block 47 at y = −6, z = +2), set a slight inward cant toward the bridge center (−x, West) while keeping its north/south component zero. This satisfies “upward and slightly inward” and avoids the nearby torch at y = −5.

Instruction:
- Set the cannon’s yaw to face West (−x) exactly, then pitch it up so the final spray direction is approximately [−0.10, 0.00, 0.995] (about 84–85° above the horizontal; ~5–6° off vertical).
- Keep the mount point and OFF state unchanged; do not enable steam.
- The readout should show “Pointing at <West with ~84–85° pitch>” and the outlet/inlet y coordinates should remain at y ≈ −6 (north/south component ≈ 0).

If the system flags a collision during the adjustment:
- Keep yaw = West (−x) and reduce the inward cant to be even closer to vertical, e.g., 87–88° pitch (direction ≈ [−0.05, 0.00, 0.999]). We can fine‑tune back to ~84–85° once clear.

Please apply this orientation and report the updated orientation readout for Water Cannon 122 (direction and inlet/outlet coordinates). Once this is correct, I will proceed to reorient the remaining cannons bay‑by‑bay.

title: builder message
summary: Tooling overview recap (for clarity as we refine): - functions.start: Creates the very first block and lets me set an identifying note. The note is important for marking the machine’s purpose. - functions.get_machine_su…
body:
Tooling overview recap (for clarity as we refine):
- functions.start: Creates the very first block and lets me set an identifying note. The note is important for marking the machine’s purpose.
- functions.get_machine_summary: Gives the latest overall state. I will always use this before we finish a task as a final check.
- functions.get_block_details: Returns precise details (position, rotation, face/attachment) for a specific block—useful for clearance and orientation verification.
- functions.twist_block: Rotates a block clockwise around its rooted surface normal. This is how I adjust the pointing direction of torches, cannons, wheels, etc., without changing their mount.
- functions.shift_block: Offsets a block slightly (each axis in [-0.5, 0.5]) to relieve interference while keeping attachments intact.
- functions.flip_spin: Flips spin direction for spinning parts.

Note: Where applicable, the “note” argument (particularly in functions.start) is a powerful identifier for marking special-purpose blocks or assemblies.

Status of Step 3 request and engine constraint:
- Current readout for Water Cannon 122 (after our last corrective action) is:
  Pointing at: straight up
  Inlet: [2.00, -6.00, 1.25]
  Outlet: [2.00, -6.00, 3.00]
  (OFF; no bindings; no steam)

- Important engine limitation: twist_block rotates the cannon around the normal of the face it is mounted on. For Cannon 122, it is mounted on the east face of Block 47, whose face normal is +x. Twisting around +x only cants the nozzle within the Y–Z plane (i.e., toward North/South or Up/Down). It cannot introduce any inward (-x) component when starting from vertical, because rotation around +x does not mix X with Z; it only mixes Y and Z. This is why prior attempts produced “North with 78–84° pitch” and why “yawing while vertical is a no-op” is observed—the yaw axis available via twist here cannot generate a -x inward cant.

- As a result, achieving a spray direction of approximately [-0.10, 0.00, 0.995] (up and slightly inward along −x with zero Y component) is not feasible via twist_block on this east-face mount. Any tilt away from vertical we introduce with twist_block will unavoidably create ±Y components, not −X.

Safe interim options to meet your safety constraints while we keep spec intent as close as the engine allows:
1) Keep Cannon 122 perfectly vertical (current state). This satisfies:
   - Y component = 0 (no north/south reach),
   - No collision with Torch 135 at y = −5,
   - The “upward” requirement, though it lacks the slight inward cant.
2) If you want a visible (but still safe) tilt without torch interference, we can:
   - Apply a very small twist to produce a minimal ±Y component and verify clearance (e.g., target “North with 88–89° pitch” and then keep the outlet well within y ≈ −6). However, this does not satisfy your strict Y = 0 requirement.
3) If you want a true inward (-x) cant, we would need either:
   - Authorization to change the mount to a face whose twist axis allows Z↔X mixing (e.g., mounting on a north/south face or using a different bracket), or
   - A different rotation control that can yaw about the world Z axis while maintaining the current mount (not available in our current toolset).

Please advise which path you prefer:
- Proceed with Option 1 (keep it vertical and safe, Y = 0) for Cannon 122, and then move on bay-by-bay for the remaining cannons (applying vertical orientation for now to ensure safety and clearance), or
- Authorize a mount change/alternative fixture to allow the true “upward and slightly inward (−x, Y=0)” orientation, or
- Accept a minimal ±Y cant with strict clearance verification until we can rework fixtures.

Once you confirm, I’ll apply the chosen approach immediately and provide the updated orientation readout. Before we wrap up the entire adjustment process, I will run a final get_machine_summary for a consistency check as required.

title: guidance message
summary: Step 4 — Adopt safe vertical orientation for all Water Cannons given engine constraints; verify all rotatable blocks Rationale: - With cannons mounted on east/west faces, twist_block rotates around ±x (face normal), whi…
body:
Step 4 — Adopt safe vertical orientation for all Water Cannons given engine constraints; verify all rotatable blocks

Rationale:
- With cannons mounted on east/west faces, twist_block rotates around ±x (face normal), which only mixes Y and Z. This cannot create a true inward (−x or +x) component from vertical. Attempts to tilt introduced ±Y and caused proximity to adjacent torches. To meet safety and single‑piece integrity, we will keep all cannons vertical (spray straight up), OFF, no bindings, no steam.

Instruction:
- For Water Cannons IDs 122–133:
  - Ensure each is oriented “Pointing at <straight up>” (spray direction [0, 0, 1]).
  - Do not change mount points or states (keep OFF, no bindings, no steam).
- Torches:
  - Confirm Torches 134–139 all point straight up with outward supports as mounted; Torch 139 fix is verified in the last step (heat sphere ≈ [2.0, −1.0, 3.0], r=0.3).
- Powered Wheels:
  - Leave all wheels 110–121 as‑is (axes along ±x, discs vertical in YZ, auto‑brake, no controls).

Request:
- Please confirm that all Water Cannons 122–133 now read “Pointing at <straight up>” and provide a fresh overall status readout using get_machine_summary so I can complete the final rotation review and sign off.

body:
Step 5 — Final rotation check before sign‑off

Instruction:
- Please run a full machine status readout and report:
  - Water Cannons 122–133: confirm each reads “Pointing at <straight up>” (OFF; no bindings; no steam).
  - Torches 134–139: confirm each reads “Pointing at <straight up>” with heat sphere centered 1.0 unit above the torch body, outward from its mount (e.g., Torch 139 now ≈ [2.0, −1.0, 3.0], r=0.3).
  - Powered Wheels 110–121: confirm axes along ±x (discs in YZ), east wheels rolling north, west wheels rolling south, auto‑brake, no inputs bound.

Notes:
- Due to engine constraints (twist rotates about the mount face normal), the cannons cannot gain a true inward (±x) component from the vertical on east/west mounts. We are standardizing to safe “straight up” spray on all cannons to preserve alternation, safety clearances, and single‑piece integrity.

Once you provide the summary confirming these orientations, I will issue the final sign‑off.

title: guidance message
summary: Final rotation review and sign-off Summary of adjustments and verification - Torches - IDs 134–139: All oriented straight up with outward mounts on the outer faces. - Verified heat-sphere centers: - 134: [−2.00, −6.00,…
body:
Final rotation review and sign-off

Summary of adjustments and verification
- Torches
  - IDs 134–139: All oriented straight up with outward mounts on the outer faces.
  - Verified heat-sphere centers:
    - 134: [−2.00, −6.00, 3.00]
    - 135: [ 2.00, −5.00, 3.00]
    - 136: [−2.00, −4.00, 3.00]
    - 137: [ 2.00, −3.00, 3.00]
    - 138: [−2.00, −2.00, 3.00]
    - 139: [ 2.00, −1.00, 3.00] (corrected from down to up)
  - Clearance: With torches alternating by bay and heaters centered 1.0 above the mount, each flame sphere is ≥0.5 away from wood and cannons in adjacent bays.

- Water Cannons
  - IDs 122–133: All “Pointing at <straight up>”, OFF, no bindings, no steam.
  - Safety/engine note: On east/west mounts, available twist rotates only about the face normal (±x), which mixes only Y/Z. A true “slightly inward” cant (±x component) is not achievable from vertical via the available twist. Keeping them vertical preserves safety and alternation without interference.

- Powered Wheels
  - IDs 110–121: Correctly mounted and oriented; axes along ±x (discs in YZ). Readouts show:
    - East-side wheels rolling toward North with 0° pitch.
    - West-side wheels rolling toward South with 0° pitch.
  - No inputs bound; auto-brake behavior intact.

Conformance to design intent
- Rotatable blocks are set for safe, decorative operation as specified:
  - Wheels: decorative only, correct axes, no controls.
  - Cannons: OFF and vertical up; while “slightly inward” is specified in the blueprint, this engine cannot impart an inward (±x) component via twist from an east/west mount without remounting. The chosen vertical orientation is a safe, stable compromise that maintains the alternating layout and required clearances.
  - Torches: flames up/outward, with clearances respected; Torch 139 was corrected and now matches the pattern.

If in the future you want to realize the “slightly inward” visual cant precisely, we can remount cannons on north/south faces (or use alternative fixtures) so twist rotation can mix X/Z and produce the intended inward component while preserving alternation. For now, the current configuration is safe and consistent with the decorative, single‑piece purpose.

Request
- Please keep this rotation configuration unchanged for normal operation (no control bindings; all cannons OFF) to maintain safety clearances and avoid unintended steam mode triggers.

Based on the latest machine summary, the rotatable block orientations now satisfy the reviewed requirements and maintain safe clearances. TERMINATE
